memory management unit (mmu) as mentioned in section 1.3.2, virtual memory is an illusion of a memory that may be larger that the real memory of a computer. as described in section 1.4.3, an os implements virtual memory by using noncontiguous memory allocation and the mmu. the os allocates a set of memory are as to a program, and stores information concerning these areas in a table of memory allocation information. during the execution of the program, the cpu passes the address of a data or instruction used in the current instruction to the mmu. this address is called a logical address. the mmu uses the memory allocation information to find the address in memory where the required data or instruction actually resides. this address is called the physical address, and the process of obtaining it from a logical address is called address translation. in the interest of simplicity, we do not describe details of address translation.

osi model the osi (open systems interconnection) model has seven layers: physical layer � transmits raw bits over physical media; defines cables, connectors, signal types, and speed. data link layer � frames data; error detection; controls access to medium; protocols include ethernet, ppp, mac. network layer � logical addressing and routing; determines the best path; ip operates here. transport layer � end-to-end delivery; segmentation; error handling; flow control; tcp/udp. session layer � manages sessions between applications; synchronizes dialog. presentation layer � converts data into standard format; encryption; compression. application layer � services for end-user applications; e.g., http, ftp, smtp, dns.

data cleaning is the process of fixing or removing incorrect, corrupted, duplicate, or incomplete data within a dataset to improve its quality, accuracy, and usability for analysis. it involves removing irrelevant observations, handling missing values, structural error correction, and standardizing formats. this essential process enhances data integrity and decision-making..

this book presents process management concepts in a structured academic manner. it explains process life cycles, scheduling algorithms, and synchronization techniques. the author discusses classical concurrency problems such as producer-consumer and readers-writers. deadlock detection and prevention strategies are explained clearly. the book also explores multiprogramming and time-sharing systems. memory management interaction with processes is described in detail. the text is useful for understanding theoretical foundations of process management.

nlp is essential in developing intelligent chatbots and virtual assistants. preprocessing prepares user input for analysis. tf-idf is used in simpler systems, while deep learning models use embeddings. transfer learning allows models to learn from large datasets and apply knowledge to new tasks. fine-tuning improves performance on specific applications. sentiment analysis helps chatbots understand user emotions, and named entity recognition extracts important information from conversations.

this widely used textbook explains process management in a structured and accessible manner. it covers process abstraction, context switching, and scheduling algorithms such as round robin and multilevel feedback queues. the book discusses threads, locks, condition variables, and semaphores for synchronization. deadlock detection and prevention techniques are explained clearly. the authors provide practical examples and experiments to reinforce learning. the text connects theoretical concepts with real-world operating system design, making it highly effective for understanding modern process management.

in circuit switching, a dedicated communication path is established between two devices before data transfer begins. in contrast, packet switching sends data in small packets without reserving a fixed path. packet switching is more efficient because network resources are shared among many users. modern internet communication relies primarily on packet switching, which enables flexible routing and dynamic traffic management.

nlp plays a critical role in applications like machine translation, voice assistants, and text summarization. preprocessing ensures data is clean and consistent. tf-idf is useful for simpler models, while deep learning models use embeddings. transfer learning allows pre-trained models to be reused for new tasks, reducing training time. fine-tuning adjusts these models for specific datasets. sentiment analysis is used in social media monitoring, and named entity recognition helps identify key entities in large text corpora.

a modem (combination of modulator/demodulator) is used to modulate the electrical signal, i.e. to change the signal so that it can be sent through a cable that uses a different technology. historically, modems were used to modulate the digital electrical signals coming from computer systems so that they could be transmitted over telephone wires in an analogue form. these were superseded by adsl modems, which connected to the same telephone lines but used new electronics at the exchange of data. as technology has improved, and to simplify connectivity for the home user, modems are now usually built into the network access device supplied by the internet service provider.

dns is an application layer protocol used to translate domain names (like www.openai.com) into ip addresses that computers can understand and use to establish communication .humans use domain names because they�re easy to remember, while machines communicate using numerical ip addresses. dns acts as the �internet�s phonebook�, helping resolve domain names to their corresponding ip addresses automatically behind the scenes. dns performs tasks such as: resolving fully qualified domain names (fqdn) to ip addresses providing mail server information (mx records) caching query results to speed up repeated lookups supporting reverse lookups (ip to domain name) dns usually operates over udp for faster performance (since it�s typically a small, single request/response), but tcp is used for larger queries like zone transfers.

memory attached to the cpu is used for storage of data and instructions and is called internal memory the internal memory is divided into many storage locations, each of which can store data or instructions. each memory location is of the same size and has an address. with the help of the address, the computer can read any memory location easily without having to search the entire memory. when a program is executed, it's data is copied to the internal memory and is stored in the memory till the end of the execution. the internal memory is also called the primary memory or main memory. this memory is also called as ram, i.e. random access memory. the time of access of data is independent of its location in memory, therefore this memory is also called random access memory (ram).

route redistribution is a process used in networks where multiple routing protocols operate simultaneously. it allows routing information learned from one protocol (such as ospf) to be shared with another protocol (such as eigrp or rip). this is useful when integrating networks that use different routing technologies. routers performing redistribution translate route metrics and advertise them to other routing domains. proper configuration is important because incorrect redistribution can cause routing loops or inefficient routing paths.
hybrid topology is created by combining two or more different network topologies. for example, a star-bus or star-ring combination. it is flexible and scalable for large network infrastructures.

segmentation an important aspect of memory management that became unavoidable with paging is the separation of the user's view of memory and the actual physical memory. the user's view of memory is not the same as the actual physical memory. the user's view is mapped onto physical memory. the mapping allows differentiation between logical memory and physical memory.

describes context switching as a key mechanism not only in single systems but also in distributed environments. in distributed operating systems, context switching enables processes to pause execution locally while awaiting communication or synchronization with remote systems. the os saves the execution state so the process can resume correctly once resources become available. context switching supports efficient resource utilization but introduces overhead, especially when network delays are involved. the book emphasizes minimizing context switches to improve distributed system performance.

a core idea in reinforcement learning is the formulation of problems using markov decision processes (mdps). an mdp consists of states, actions, transition probabilities, and reward functions. the markov property implies that the future state depends only on the current state and action, not on past history. this simplification allows efficient computation of optimal policies. value functions are used to estimate expected future rewards from a given state, while policy functions determine the best actions. algorithms like value iteration and policy iteration rely heavily on this framework to iteratively improve decision-making strategies.

layer 3: network layer the network layer works for the transmission of data from one host to the other located in different networks. it also takes care of packet routing i.e. selection of the shortest path to transmit the packet, from the number of routes available. the sender and receiver's ip address are placed in the header by the network layer. segment in the network layer is referred to as packet. network layer is implemented by networking devices such as routers and switches.

the future of generative models lies in multimodal learning, where models handle text, images, and audio together. a key subtopic is scalability, which enables training on massive datasets. these advancements will drive the next generation of ai systems.

network components: nic (network interface card): allows devices to connect to a network. router: connects different networks and determines optimal paths for data. switch: connects devices within the same network and forwards data to specific devices. hub: connects multiple devices in lan; broadcasts data to all devices. gateway: connects networks with different protocols.

natural language processing enables intelligent systems. preprocessing ensures data quality. tf-idf highlights important words. transfer learning reduces training effort. fine-tuning adapts models to tasks. sentiment analysis detects emotions, and named entity recognition identifies entities.

this edition explains process scheduling policies, thread management, and synchronization techniques in structured academic style. it compares preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling methods. deadlock modeling and resource allocation techniques are described with diagrams. the book is especially useful for academic understanding of os fundamentals.

the transport layer manages communication between end systems and ensures that data reaches the correct application process. tcp provides reliable communication through techniques such as acknowledgments, retransmission of lost packets, and sequencing of segments. udp offers a lightweight communication method without connection establishment. flow control ensures that data transmission does not overwhelm the receiving host, while congestion control prevents network congestion by adjusting the transmission rate. port numbers are essential for identifying application processes on a device, allowing multiple network services to operate simultaneously without interference.

ensemble learning improves model performance by combining predictions. bagging reduces variance, boosting focuses on errors, gradient boosting optimizes loss functions, and xgboost improves speed and accuracy.

file protection in operating systems extends beyond basic permissions to safeguard files against unauthorized access, modification, or deletion through layered mechanisms like access control lists (acls), mandatory access control (mac), encryption, and integrity checks, ensuring data confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

direct memory access direct memory access (dma) is a feature of computer systems that allows certain hardware subsystems to access main system memory (random-access memory) independent of the central processing unit (cpu). without dma, when the cpu is using programmed input/output, it is typically fully occupied for the entire duration of the read or write operation, and is thus unavailable to perform other work. with dma, the cpu first initiates the transfer, then it does other operations while the transfer is in progress, and it finally receives an interrupt from the dma controller (dmac) when the operation is done. this feature is useful at any time that the cpu cannot keep up with the rate of data transfer, or when the cpu needs to perform work while waiting for a relatively slow i/o data transfer. many hardware systems use dma, including disk drive https://eng.libretexts.org/@go/page/82923 16.2.2 controllers, graphics cards, network cards and sound cards. dma is also used for intra-chip data transfer in multi-core processors. computers that have dma channels can transfer data to and from devices with much less cpu overhead than computers without dma channels. similarly, a processing element inside a multi-core processor can transfer data to and from its local memory without occupying its processor time, allowing computation and data transfer to proceed in parallel. dma can also be used for "memory to memory" copying or moving of data within memory. dma can offload expensive memory operations, such as large copies or scatter-gather operations, from the cpu to a dedicated dma engine. an implementation example is the i/o acceleration technology. dma is of interest in network-on-chip and in-memory computing architectures

fixed partitioning: multi-programming with fixed partitioning is a contiguous memory management approach that splits the main memory into fixed sized segments that can be of equal or unequal size. this method is used in conjunction with multi-programming with dynamic partitioning. this method is included in the multi-programming with fixed partitioning approach, which is discussed further down in this section. when we need to allocate memory for a process, we search for a free partition that is large enough to contain the process that we are allocating memory for. when we find a partition that meets these requirements, we then allocate memory for the process. after this, the ram will be put to the side for the subsequent operation. in the case that there is not sufficient free space available, the process will wait in the queue in order to be assigned memory. this occurs when there is not enough free space available. it is one of the methods for memory management that has been around the longest and is easy to put into practice. it is also one of the most effective methods.

the data link layer focuses on providing reliable communication between neighboring devices. framing divides transmitted data into frames with clearly defined boundaries. error detection and correction mechanisms protect data from corruption during transmission. flow control is used to manage the speed of communication between sender and receiver. mac addressing ensures that each device on the network has a unique identifier. through switching concepts, network switches analyze destination mac addresses and forward frames accordingly, allowing efficient communication between devices connected to the network.

it is important to note that a processor can only access memory one location at a time, so the vast majority of ram is unused at any moment. since disk space is cheap compared to ram, then moving information in ram to hard disk can greatly expand ram space at no cost. this technique is called virtual memory management. disk storage is only one of the memory types that must be managed by the operating system, and is the slowest. ranked in order of speed, the types of memory in a computer system are: 1. 2. 3. high-speed cache: this is fast, relatively small amounts of memory that are available to the cpu through the fastest connections. cache controllers predict which pieces of data the cpu will need next and pull it from main memory into high speed cache to speed up system performance. main memory: this is the ram that you see measured in megabytes when you buy a computer. secondary memory: this is most often some sort of rotating magnetic storage that keeps applications and data available to be used, and serves as virtual ram under the control of the operating system. the operating system must balance the needs of the various processes with the availability of the different types of memories, moving data in blocks (called pages) between available memories as the schedule of processes dictates. systems for managing memory can be divided into two categories: the system of moving processes back and forth between main memory and disk during execution (known as swapping and paging) and the process that does not do so.

this book explains advanced process management topics such as distributed scheduling, process migration, and load balancing. it discusses fault tolerance and reliability in multiprocessor systems. synchronization algorithms for distributed environments are analyzed. the text emphasizes scalable system design.

priority scheduling is a cpu scheduling technique in which each process is assigned a priority, and the cpu executes the process with the highest priority first. in priority scheduling, the cpu runs the process with the highest priority first. it can stop a process or let it finish before switching. each process is assigned a priority number by the system or user. higher priority means the process gets the cpu sooner.

unsupervised learning is grouping fruits based on similarity in color, size, and taste, without knowing what the fruits are. common unsupervised learning algorithms include clustering methods such as kmeans, hierarchical clustering, and dimensionality reduction techniques such as principal component analysis (pca).

the file transfer protocol is another important application layer protocol used to transfer files between computers on a network. ftp operates using a client-server architecture where a user connects to a remote server and uploads or downloads files. the protocol uses two separate connections: a control connection for commands and a data connection for transferring files. ftp allows users to perform operations such as listing directories, uploading files, downloading files, and deleting files on remote systems. although ftp was widely used in earlier internet applications, it does not provide strong encryption by default. for this reason, secure alternatives such as ftps and sftp are often used in modern networks.

ensemble learning techniques are widely used because they provide more reliable predictions compared to individual models. bagging focuses on reducing variance by training multiple models on randomly sampled data subsets and averaging their outputs. this makes it particularly effective for unstable models. boosting takes a different approach by building models sequentially and emphasizing data points that were previously misclassified. this iterative correction leads to improved accuracy. gradient boosting formalizes boosting by using gradient descent to minimize a differentiable loss function, making it highly flexible. xgboost is an advanced version that includes regularization, efficient tree construction, and the ability to handle missing values, making it one of the most powerful tools in machine learning today.

this latest edition updates process management with multicore and virtualization concepts. it explains thread scheduling, hypervisors, and process isolation in virtual environments. the completely fair scheduler and real-time scheduling policies are discussed. synchronization techniques are expanded to include modern lock-free and wait-free algorithms. distributed process coordination and container-based isolation are also briefly introduced.

regularization techniques prevent deep networks from overfitting. l1 and l2 regularization penalize large weights, dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training, and data augmentation expands the training set. proper regularization ensures models generalize well to unseen data, improving reliability and performance across tasks.

synchronization is necessary because even simple operations like incrementing a shared variable involve multiple cpu instructions. without locking, concurrent threads may overwrite each other's updates. locks ensure mutual exclusion, while condition variables allow threads to wait for specific conditions without consuming cpu cycles. the text emphasizes scalability, showing that synchronization design must minimize contention in multicore systems.

this book offers comprehensive coverage of linux process management. it explains how the kernel represents processes using task_struct and how scheduling decisions are made. the authors discuss process creation, copy-on-write memory, and signal handling. detailed explanations are given for system calls, interrupt handling, and kernel synchronization mechanisms. the interaction between processes and memory management is thoroughly explored. the book provides both conceptual explanations and implementation details, helping readers understand linux multitasking at the system level.

a clustering is used to group similar data points together. clustering algorithms work by repeatedly moving data points closer to the center of their group (cluster) and farther from points in other groups. this helps the algorithm to create clear and meaningful clusters.

the dhcp enables clients on a network to obtain ip addresses and other information from a dhcp server. the protocol automates the assignment of ip addresses, subnet masks, gateway, and other ip networking parameters. dhcp allows a host to obtain an ip address dynamically when it connects to the network. the dhcp server is contacted by sending a request, and an ip address is requested. the dhcp server chooses an address from a configured range of addresses called a pool and assigns it to the host client for a set period.

precision measures how many predicted positive instances are actually correct. it is particularly important in applications like fraud detection, where false positives can be costly. high precision indicates fewer false alarms.

this book approaches process management from a system design perspective. it explains how operating systems structure process abstractions and manage concurrency in both single-processor and multiprocessor systems. the author discusses kernel-level versus user-level threads and compares scheduling policies in terms of fairness and efficiency. it explains synchronization mechanisms such as semaphores, monitors, and message passing, along with critical section problems. special emphasis is placed on system design trade-offs, scalability, and performance optimization. the text also explores real-world kernel implementations to illustrate how process scheduling and context switching are implemented internally.

burst time is defined as the time spent for executing the activity in the burst excluding the wait time in the queue. hence, ta time can also be defined as the sum of all cpu bursts and i/o bursts and wait-times in different queues. ta time is any positive real number usually expressed in microseconds, milli-seconds or seconds. waiting time: a process has to wait for any resources if there is a high demand for the resource. every resource is generally associated with a queue (fig 2.20), where processes wait to access the resource. waiting time is the time spent in the queue for the resource starting from joining the queue to using the resource. in cpu scheduling, waiting time means waiting in the cpu ready queue, unless otherwise mentioned.
the confusion matrix provides a complete overview of classification performance. it helps in identifying the types of errors made by the model and serves as the basis for many evaluation metrics.

the data link layer supports reliable communication between devices connected through the same physical medium. framing structures transmitted bits into frames with headers and trailers. error detection and correction techniques verify the accuracy of transmitted frames. flow control ensures stable communication by regulating transmission speed. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices in a local network. switching concepts allow switches to maintain mac address tables and forward frames efficiently.
physical layer: guided transmission media, wireless transmission media, switching

feature engineering involves preparing data for machine learning. feature selection removes redundant features. feature extraction creates new features. dimensionality reduction simplifies data. handling imbalanced datasets improves model performance.

a decision tree works by breaking down data step by step asking the best possible questions at each point and stopping once it reaches a clear decision. it's an easy and understandable way to make choices. because of their simple and clear structure decision trees are very helpful in machine learning for tasks like sorting data into categories or making predictions.earning

overfitting is a challenge where models memorize training data. a key subtopic is generalization, ensuring that models can generate new samples. regularization techniques such as dropout help improve performance.

the transport layer in computer networks is responsible for providing reliable communication between end systems. one of the most important protocols operating at this layer is transmission control protocol. tcp provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data between applications. before transmitting data, tcp establishes a connection using a process known as the three-way handshake. this mechanism ensures that both sender and receiver are ready for communication. tcp divides large messages into smaller segments, assigns sequence numbers, and waits for acknowledgments from the receiver. if packets are lost, tcp retransmits them automatically. these mechanisms allow applications such as http and https to exchange data reliably across the internet.

in a ring topology, each device is connected to exactly two others, forming a closed loop. data travels in one direction around the ring (or in both directions when using dual rings). each node receives data and passes it to the next until it reaches its destination. while ring topology can perform better than bus under heavy load, a single device failure can disrupt the entire ring unless special redundancy mechanisms are used.

cpu scheduling is the task performed by the cpu that decides the way and order in which processes should be executed. there are two types of cpu scheduling - preemptive, and non-preemptive. the criteria the cpu takes into consideration while "scheduling" these processes are - cpu utilization, throughput, turnaround time, waiting time, and response time.

security is an essential requirement in modern web communication, which led to the development of hypertext transfer protocol secure. https enhances http by incorporating encryption technologies such as transport layer security. this encryption protects the confidentiality and integrity of data exchanged between clients and servers. https also supports authentication through digital certificates issued by trusted certificate authorities. these certificates verify the identity of the server and help prevent malicious attacks such as man-in-the-middle attacks. because of increasing security concerns, https has become the standard protocol used by most modern websites for secure communication.

the data link layer provides reliable delivery of frames between two directly connected nodes. framing converts the continuous stream of bits into frames that include synchronization information, addressing, and error-checking fields. error detection and correction mechanisms ensure data integrity, commonly using checksums or crc codes. if errors occur during transmission, protocols may request retransmission of the affected frame. flow control techniques are used to prevent buffer overflow at the receiver by controlling the rate of transmission. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices within the same network segment. in switching concepts, switches build mac address tables dynamically and use them to forward frames only to the relevant destination ports.

file system security: the file system must provide security mechanisms to protect files and directories from unauthorized access or modification. this can be done by setting file permissions, access control lists, or encryption. file system maintenance: the file system must be maintained to ensure efficient and reliable operation. this includes tasks such as disk defragmentation, disk checking, and backup and recovery.

reliable frame transmission across physical links depends on the services provided by the data link layer. framing structures transmitted bits into frames containing addressing and control information. error detection and correction methods identify corrupted frames and ensure that only valid data is accepted by the receiver. flow control regulates the transmission rate to prevent congestion. mac addressing uniquely identifies network devices connected to a lan. switching concepts allow switches to route frames efficiently based on destination mac addresses.

the data link layer is responsible for managing communication between devices that share a physical network link. one of its main responsibilities is framing, which divides the continuous stream of bits from the physical layer into structured frames. each frame contains control information, including source and destination addresses. error detection and correction mechanisms help ensure that transmitted frames remain accurate despite noise or interference. techniques like cyclic redundancy check are widely used. flow control prevents the sender from transmitting data faster than the receiver can process it. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device in a local network. using switching concepts, switches analyze mac addresses and forward frames efficiently.

paged address translation: we examine a single model of address translation in detail: the one used by the original pentium, and by any intel-compatible cpu running in 32-bit mode. it uses 32-bit virtual addresses, 32-bit physical addresses, and a page size of 4096 bytes. since pages are 2 12 bytes each, addresses can be divided into 20-bit page numbers and 12-bit offsets within each page. the memory management unit (mmu) maps a 20-bit virtual page number to a 20 bit physical page number; the offset can pass through unchanged, giving the physical address the cpu should access. although paged address translation is far more flexible than base and bounds registers, it requires much more information. base and bounds translation only requires two values, which can easily be held in registers in the mmu. in contrast, paged translation must be able to handle a separate mapping value for each of over a million virtual pages. (although most programs will only map a fraction of those pages) the only possible place to store the amount of information required by paged address translation is in memory itself, so the mmu uses page tables in memory to specify virtual-to-physical mappings.

this advanced text analyzes linux kernel internals related to process management. it explains scheduler design, task states, and load balancing strategies. the book discusses preemption, real-time scheduling, and smp (symmetric multiprocessing). synchronization mechanisms and interrupt handling are covered in depth. the author also explores performance optimization and scalability. the book provides detailed insight into enterprise-level linux multitasking systems.

spelling and grammar checking all spelling checkers can flag words which aren't in a dictionary. (1) (2) * the necessary steps are obvious. the necessary steps are obvious. if the user can expand the dictionary, or if the language has complex productive morphology (see §2.1), then a simple list of words isn't enough to do this and some morphological processing is needed.3 more subtle cases involve words which are correct in isolation, but not in context. syntax could sort some of these cases out. for instance, possessive its generally has to be immediately followed by a noun or by one or more adjectives which are immediately in front of a noun.

data encoding is an important pre-processing step in machine learning. it refers to the process of converting categorical or textual data into numerical format, so that it can be used as input for algorithms to process. the reason for encoding is that most machine learning algorithms work with numbers and not with text or categorical variables.

in supervised learning, evaluating model performance goes beyond simple correctness. the roc curve is a graphical representation that illustrates the trade-off between true positive rate and false positive rate. the area under the curve (auc) summarizes this performance into a single value. a higher auc indicates better model discrimination. another important concept is bias-variance tradeoff, which explains the balance between model simplicity and flexibility. high bias leads to underfitting, while high variance results in overfitting. effective evaluation techniques aim to strike a balance between these two extremes.

in networking systems, the transport layer provides communication services that allow applications to exchange data across networks. tcp provides reliable data transfer by establishing a connection between sender and receiver. it ensures data integrity through error detection and retransmission mechanisms. udp provides a simpler communication method that sends data without establishing a connection. flow control mechanisms regulate the speed of data transmission so that the receiver can process incoming segments effectively. congestion control algorithms monitor network conditions and reduce transmission rates during congestion. port numbers play a key role in identifying services such as web browsing, email communication, and file transfer.

a ring topology is a network configuration in which device connections create a circular data path. in a ring network, packets of data travel from one device to the next until they reach their destination. most ring topologies allow packets to travel only in one direction, called a unidirectional ring network. others permit data to move in either direction, called bidirectional. the major disadvantage of a ring topology is that if any individual connection in the ring is broken, the entire network is affected. ring topologies may be used in either local area networks (lans) or wide area networks (wans).

network security is implemented through multiple, coordinated layers of control rather than a single defensive mechanism. at a foundational level, network security combines technologies such as firewalls, intrusion detection and prevention systems (ids/ips), network segmentation, vpns, and ddos protection. while each control serves a distinct purpose, together they help enforce policy, monitor traffic, and reduce the attack surface across the network. effective network security strategies emphasize defense in depth, ensuring that failures at one layer do not expose the entire environment.

this text describes context switching as the fundamental mechanism enabling time-sharing systems. the operating system regains control through interrupts and determines whether to continue executing the current process or switch to another. the switching procedure involves saving the complete processor context of the outgoing process and loading the incoming process's context from its pcb. the authors explain that context switching cost depends on hardware architecture, memory hierarchy, and scheduler implementation. frequent switching can negatively affect cache performance and throughput, but it improves responsiveness in interactive workloads.

information gain tells us how useful a question (or feature) is for splitting data into groups. it measures how much the uncertainty decreases after the split. a good question will create clearer groups and the feature with the highest information gain is chosen to make the decision.

storage management storage devices. chapter 12 surveys block storage hardware, specifically magnetic disks and flash memory. the last two decades have seen rapid change in storage technology affecting both application programmers and operating systems designers; this chapter provides a snapshot for students, as a building block for the next two chapters. if students have previously seen this material, this chapter can be skipped.

there are three main types of schedulers, each performing different tasks in processmanagement: long-term (job) scheduler : moves processes from secondary memory (job pool) to main memory (ready queue). medium-term scheduler : suspends and resumes processes based on system status. short-term (cpu) scheduler : selects one of the ready processes in memory to execute next. starvation (or indefinite blocking) occurs in priority scheduling when a low-priority process keeps waiting indefinitely because higher-priority processes continuously get the cpu. this problem arises in heavily loaded systems where resources are always occupied by higher-priority tasks, leaving some processes starved. to prevent this, operating systems use aging, a technique that gradually increases the priority of waiting processes, ensuring fair execution. causes of starvation not fair scheduling: if there are always higher-priority processes available, then the lowerpriority processes may never be allowed to run. apart from priority, there can be other causes like a scheduling algorithm that randomly picks a process and somehow a victim process is always missed leading to starvation. limited resources : due to limitation of resources one or the other process has to wait. aging is a scheduling technique used to prevent starvation by gradually increasing the priority of processes waiting too long in the system. ensures fairness, as long-waiting processes eventually get cpu time. often combined with algorithms like priority scheduling or round-robin to balance shortterm efficiency with long-term fairness. for example, if priorities range from 127 (low) to 0 (high), a waiting process can move up one level every 15 minutes, ensuring even the lowest-priority process eventually gets executed. cpu scheduling is a core os function that determines which process runs on the cpu, maximizing utilization, throughput, and fairness while minimizing wait/response times. it manages multitasking by switching the cpu between processes in the ready queue, using algorithms like fcfs, round robin, and priority scheduling to balance efficiency and system performance.

network models divide communication into layers to simplify networking tasks such as addressing, routing, error detection, and data formatting. each layer performs specific functions and interacts only with adjacent layers, making troubleshooting and development easier.
ensemble learning is a powerful machine learning approach. bagging reduces variance, boosting reduces bias, gradient boosting optimizes loss, and xgboost enhances speed and accuracy.

in network communication systems, the data link layer plays a significant role in ensuring that frames are transmitted accurately across a physical link. framing encapsulates data from the network layer into frames with defined boundaries. these frames include control information used for synchronization and addressing. error detection and correction mechanisms detect corrupted frames caused by noise or interference in the transmission medium. flow control is implemented to coordinate the transmission rate between communicating devices. mac addressing uniquely identifies each device in a local network environment. using switching concepts, switches learn mac addresses dynamically and forward frames to the correct port, thereby reducing network congestion and improving communication efficiency.

in modern computer networks, the network layer provides routing and addressing services that enable communication between hosts on different networks. logical addressing is implemented through internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv6 was introduced to overcome the limitations of ipv4 and to support the rapid expansion of the internet. subnetting allows network administrators to divide networks into smaller logical units, improving traffic control and security. routing algorithms evaluate available paths and select the most efficient one based on metrics such as hop count or delay. routing protocols like routing information protocol and open shortest path first enable routers to exchange routing tables. on a global scale, border gateway protocol manages routing between autonomous systems.

ddos attacks: distributed denial of service attacks flood networks with traffic, disrupting access. online-dependent firms suffer significant revenue losses during such outages. man-in-the-middle (mitm) attacks: attackers intercept communications to steal data or inject malicious payloads, often on unsecured networks. sql injection: exploits database vulnerabilities to manipulate or extract information, targeting poorly secured applications.

c-scan scheduling circular scan (c-scan) scheduling is a variant of scan designed to provide a more uniform wait time. like scan, c-scan moves the head from one end of the disk to the other, servicing requests along the way. when the head reaches the other end, however, it immediately returns to the beginning of the disk without servicing any requests on the return trip (figure 10.7). the c-scan scheduling algorithm essentially treats the cylinders as a circular list that wraps around from the final cylinder to the first one.

the data link layer supports reliable frame delivery across physical connections. framing structures raw data into frames with defined boundaries. error detection and correction mechanisms ensure that transmission errors are detected. flow control synchronizes the transmission rate between communicating devices. mac addressing provides unique identification for devices in a network. switching concepts enable switches to forward frames selectively to reduce network congestion.

computer networking is the backbone of the internet and all digital communication. from transferring files and sending emails to hosting websites and cloud applications, none of it works without solid networking infrastructure. that's why understanding networking is nonnegotiable for it students, developers, cybersecurity professionals, and network engineers.

this book presents process management from both conceptual and implementation viewpoints. it defines a process as an executing instance of a program, including its address space, execution context, and system resources. the author explains process state transitions, the role of the process control block (pcb), and context switching overhead. scheduling strategies such as first-come-first-served (fcfs), shortest job first (sjf), priority scheduling, and multilevel feedback queues are analyzed with performance metrics like turnaround time and response time. the book also explains interprocess communication (ipc), synchronization primitives, and deadlock management techniques including prevention, avoidance, and detection methods.

feature engineering plays a central role in large-scale machine learning systems. feature selection uses hybrid approaches combining filter and wrapper methods for optimal feature subsets. feature extraction includes advanced techniques such as embeddings for text or deep feature representations for images. dimensionality reduction methods like pca and t-sne are used for both compression and visualization. handling imbalanced datasets involves ensemble approaches like balanced random forests and boosting techniques, which improve performance on minority classes without sacrificing overall accuracy.

because of the time it can take to format a tape, typically tapes are pre-formatted so that the tape user does not need to spend time preparing each new tape for use. all that is usually necessary is to write an identifying media label to the tape before use, and even this can be automatically written by software when a new tape is used for the first time. another concept for file management is the idea of a database-based file system. instead of, or in addition to, hierarchical structured management, files are identified by their characteristics, like type of file, topic, author, or similar rich metadata.

a file system in an operating system (os) serves as the crucial intermediary between users/applications and storage hardware, organizing data into files and directories while managing storage allocation, access control, and efficient retrieval to prevent fragmentation and ensure reliability. it comprises key components like hierarchical directory structures that link file names to metadata (such as inodes in unix-like systems), per-file control blocks (fcbs) storing details like size, location, and permissions, open-file tables (both system-wide and per-process) for tracking active files via descriptors or indices, and mount tables for handling multiple volumes. common types include ntfs for windows with journaling and encryption, ext4 for linux supporting large files via extents, apfs for macos optimized for ssds with snapshots, and fat32 for broad compatibility though lacking advanced recovery features.

networking environments depend on the data link layer to organize frame transmission across network links. framing groups transmitted bits into frames with control information. error detection and correction mechanisms verify frame integrity. flow control regulates communication speeds. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices in the network. switching concepts allow switches to deliver frames accurately.

endpoint detection and response (edr): endpoint detection and response (edr) security solutions continuously monitor all user and endpoint activities to protect them from threats and detect suspicious behavior. they also offer investigation and incident response capabilities, which eradicate the threat and isolate the affected system from impacting the rest of the network.

shared memory techniques only require a one-time performance penalty during the set-up phase. once the memory has been shared, there is no additional penalty, regardless of the amount of data transferred. the trade-off is that the penalty for setting up shared memory is significant. the kernel must perform several slow operations to link the shared region to all of the processes' virtual memory spaces.

when the cpu becomes idle, the process selection is made by a short-term scheduler (or cpu scheduler). the scheduler selects from those ready to execute and allocates the cpu to that process. the cpu scheduling takes place in these process state change situations. dispatcher the dispatcher is the module that gives control of the cpu to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.

file management a file is a collection of related information defined by its creator. commonly, files represent programs (both source and object forms) and data. the operating system is responsible for the following activities in connections with file management: file creation and deletion.  directory creation and deletion. support of primitives for manipulating files and directories.  mapping files onto secondary storage.  file backup on stable (nonvolatile) storage media.

in this model, each process is assigned a priority number (e.g., 0 to 10). the cpu always works on the process with the highest priority. in a preemptive version, if a high-priority task arrives while a lower-priority one is running, the cpu switches immediately. in non-preemptive, it finishes the current task first. the main risk here is that low-priority background tasks might never run. to fix this, systems use aging, which gradually increases the priority of a process the longer it stays in the waiting queue.

classification has traditionally been a type of supervised machine learning, which means it uses labeled data to train models. in supervised learning, each data point in the training data contains input variables (also known as independent variables or features), and an output variable, or label.

reinforcement learning distinguishes between model-based and model-free approaches. model-based methods assume knowledge of the environment's transition dynamics, allowing planning through simulation. in contrast, model-free methods learn directly from experience without building an explicit model. q-learning is a widely used model-free algorithm that learns the value of state-action pairs. it updates estimates using temporal difference learning, which combines ideas from dynamic programming and monte carlo methods. these techniques allow agents to learn efficiently even when the environment is unknown or complex.

i/o systems one of the purposes of an operating system is to hide the peculiarities of specific hardware devices from the user. for example, in unix, the peculiarities of i/o devices are hidden from the bulk of the operating system itself by the i/o subsystem. the i/o subsystem consists of several components: a memory-management component that includes buffering, caching, and spooling a general device-driver interface drivers for specific hardware devices only the device driver knows the peculiarities of the specific device to which it is assigned. we discussed in section 1.2.3 how interrupt handlers and device drivers are used in the construction of efficient i/o subsystems. in chapter 13, we discuss how the i/o subsystem interfaces to the other system components, manages devices, transfers data, and detects i/o completion.

variational inference is used to approximate complex probability distributions that are difficult to compute directly. a key subtopic is kl divergence, which measures the difference between true and approximate distributions. this technique is essential in models like variational autoencoders, enabling efficient training.

lasso regression performs both feature selection and regularization. this means that lasso will encourage sparsity by shrinking less important feature coefficients towards zero and effectively "pruning" irrelevant features.
network communication rests on these physical foundations. it explains network topologies, transmission media, and data transmission modes.
ensemble learning improves predictions by combining models. bagging reduces variance, boosting focuses on errors, gradient boosting uses gradient descent, and xgboost improves efficiency.

this book explains process and task management in embedded systems. it describes preemptive scheduling, interrupt-driven execution, and resource-constrained multitasking. synchronization primitives such as semaphores and event flags are explained in embedded contexts. emphasis is placed on reliability and timing guarantees.

the data link layer provides reliable node-to-node communication between devices connected through the same physical medium. one of its fundamental tasks is framing, which organizes the continuous stream of bits from the physical layer into structured frames. these frames contain headers and trailers that carry addressing and control information. error detection and correction techniques ensure that corrupted frames caused by transmission noise can be identified and retransmitted. flow control manages the rate at which frames are transmitted so that the receiving device is not overwhelmed. mac addressing uniquely identifies each network interface in a local network. switching concepts allow switches to examine destination mac addresses and forward frames to the correct port.

this book explains process management in distributed systems. it discusses distributed synchronization, logical clocks, consensus protocols, and distributed deadlock detection. the authors explain how processes coordinate across networked nodes. the book emphasizes reliability, fault tolerance, and scalability.

file system is a data structure used by an operating system to manage files and directories in an organised manner. the file system directory connects users data and information to a software oriented storage. a distributed file system provides file access and orientation between shared network computers. with file systems users can share multiple data information through storage devices.

natural language processing has advanced with transformers. preprocessing ensures clean input. tf-idf is used for baseline models. self-attention captures word relationships. bert is used for understanding, and gpt for generation. sentiment analysis detects sentiment, and named entity recognition identifies entities.

reinforcement learning with transformers integrates attention mechanisms to model long sequences of states and actions. this approach improves decision-making in environments with complex temporal dependencies. applications include strategic games, robotics, and simulation environments, where understanding long-term consequences is critical for optimal policy learning.

the network layer plays a critical role in enabling communication between different networks by providing logical addressing and routing mechanisms. devices connected to the internet are assigned addresses using either internet protocol version 4 or internet protocol version 6. ipv4 uses a 32-bit address format that allows around four billion unique addresses, while ipv6 uses a 128-bit format to support the massive growth of internet-connected devices. subnetting is widely used to divide large networks into smaller subnetworks, which improves performance and simplifies management. routing algorithms determine the most efficient path for packet delivery by analyzing network topology and link costs. routing protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first allow routers to exchange routing information. for inter-network routing across autonomous systems, border gateway protocol is commonly used.

sniffing and snooping. an important step in many network attacks concerns the interception of network packets. certainly if sensitive information is sent ��in the clear�� (without encryption), the ability to intercept network traffic is very useful for the attacker, but even encrypted traffic can be useful�to find out the mac addresses of communicating parties, who talks to whom and when, etc. moreover, an attacker needs to intercept the encrypted traffic to break the encryption.

methods for handling deadlocks generally speaking, we can deal with the deadlock problem in one of three ways:  we can use a protocol to prevent or avoid deadlocks, ensuring that the system will never enter a deadlocked state.  we can allow the system to enter a deadlocked state, detect it, and recover.  we can ignore the problem altogether and pretend that deadlocks never occur in the system.

structure of the page table 1) hierarchical paging 2) hashed page-tables 3) inverted page-tables 3.14.1 hierarchical paging  problem: most computers support a large logical-address space (232 to 264). in these systems, the page-table itself becomes excessively large. solution: divide the page-table into smaller pieces. two level paging algorithm  the page-table itself is also paged (figure 3.22).  this is also known as a forward-mapped page-table because address translation works from the outer page-table inwards.  for example (figure 3.23):  consider the system with a 32-bit logical-address space and a page-size of 4 kb. a logical-address is divided into  20-bit page-number and  12-bit page-offset.  since the page-table is paged, the page-number is further divided into  10-bit page-number and  10-bit page-offset.
linear and logistic regression are often the starting points for supervised learning. linear regression seeks to find a straight-line relationship between variables to predict a specific number.

a computer network is a collection of interconnected devices, such as computers, servers, printers, and routers, that communicate with each other to share resources, exchange data, and provide services. networks can vary in size, from a small local area network (lan) within a single office or building to a wide area network (wan) that spans cities, countries, or even continents. networks allow users to share files, printers, software applications, and internet connections efficiently, improving both collaboration and resource utilization.
natural language processing (nlp): in natural language processing, unsupervised learning techniques are used for tasks such as topic modeling, document clustering, and building ai language models.

deadlock detection  if a system does not use either deadlock-prevention or deadlock-avoidance algorithm then a deadlock may occur.  in this environment, the system must provide 1) an algorithm to examine the systemstate to determine whether a deadlock has occurred. 2) an algorithm to recover from the deadlock. 3.7.1 single instance of each resource type  if all the resources have only a single instance, then deadlock detectionalgorithm can be defined using a wait-for-graph.  the wait-for-graph is applicable to only a single instance of a resource type.  a wait-for-graph (wag) is a variation of the resource-allocation-graph.  the wait-for-graph can be obtained from the resource-allocation-graph by  removing the resource nodes and  collapsing the appropriate edges.  an edge from pi to pj implies that process pi is waiting for process pj to release a resource that pi needs.  an edge pi  pj exists if and only if the corresponding graph contains two edges 1) pi  rq and 2) rq  pj. a deadlock exists in the system if and only if the wait-for-graph contains a cycle.  to detect deadlocks, the system needs to  maintain the wait-for-graph and  periodically execute an algorithm that searches for a cycle in the graph.

(mmu) in processors such as the cortex-a53. while memory itself is remarkably straightforward, os architects have built lots of abstraction layers on top. principally, these abstractions serve to improve performance and/or programmability. for both the administrator and the programmer, it is important to have a good understanding of how the memory system works and what its performance trade-offs are. this is tightly connected with concepts such as virtual memory, paging, swap space, etc. the programmer also needs to understand how memory is allocated and what the memory protection mechanisms are. all this is covered in chapter 6, memory management.' we briefly review caches (in hardware and software) to improve access speed. we go into detail about virtual memory to improve the management of physical memory resource. we will provide highly graphical descriptions of address translation, paging, page tables, page faults, swapping, etc. we explore standard schemes for page replacement, copy-on-write, etc. we will examine concrete examples in arm architecture and linux os.

nlp systems use prompt engineering and deployment pipelines. preprocessing ensures clean input. few-shot prompting improves performance. chain-of-thought enhances reasoning. hallucination affects accuracy. bias and fairness are important considerations.

peer entity authentication: verifies the identities of the peer entities involved in communication. provides use at time of mediaconnectionestblishment and during data transmission. provides confidence against a masquera or replay attack data origin authentication: assumes the authenticity of source of data unit, but does not provide protection against duplication or modification of data units. supports applications like electronic mail, where no prior interactions take place between communicating entities.

anderson and dahlin provide a detailed explanation of context switching in modern systems. they describe how the scheduler and dispatcher collaborate to switch cpu control between threads. subtopics include kernel stacks, interrupt frames, processor state saving, and memory address space switching. the book explains performance implications such as tlb shootdowns, cache pollution, and scheduling granularity. it also discusses how multiprocessor systems handle load balancing and processor affinity to reduce switching overhead.

ipv6 replaces broadcast communication with multicast mechanisms that allow efficient delivery of packets to multiple hosts simultaneously. multicast groups are identified by special ipv6 addresses, and routers forward packets only to network segments containing interested receivers. this reduces unnecessary traffic and improves bandwidth efficiency for applications such as streaming media and online conferencing.

page replacement in our presentation so far, the page-fault rate has not been a serious problem, because each page has faulted at most once, when it is first referenced. this representation is not strictly accurate. if a process of ten pages actually uses only one-half of them, then demand paging saves the i/o necessary to load the five pages that are never used. we could also increase our degree of multiprogramming by running twice as many processes. thus, if we had 40 frames, we could run eight processes, rather than the four that could run if each required 10 frames (five of which were never used). if we increase our degree of multiprogramming, we are over-allocating memory. if we run six processes, each of which is ten pages in size, but actually uses only five pages, we have higher cpu utilization and throughput, with 10 frames to spare. it is possible, however, that each of these processes, for a particular data set, may suddenly try to use all ten of its pages, resulting in a need for 60 frames, when only 40 are available. although this situation may be unlikely, it becomes much more likely as we increase the multiprogramming level, so that the average memory usage is close to the available physical memory.

access control lists are rule sets used in routers to filter packets based on criteria such as source ip address, destination ip address, protocol type, or port number. acls allow administrators to control which traffic is permitted or denied across the network. for example, a router can block traffic from unauthorized ip addresses or restrict access to sensitive resources. acls operate at the network layer by examining packet headers and applying security policies. they play an important role in network security, traffic management, and policy enforcement within enterprise networks.

the data link layer provides structured communication across a physical network medium. its framing function groups bits into frames that include synchronization patterns and addressing fields. these frames help maintain proper communication between sender and receiver. error detection and correction mechanisms ensure data integrity, often using crc algorithms to detect corrupted frames. flow control protocols regulate transmission rates and prevent network congestion. mac addressing is essential in local area networks because it allows devices to be uniquely identified at the hardware level. switching concepts involve forwarding frames based on mac addresses, enabling efficient communication between devices connected to a network switch.

feature engineering enhances data quality. feature selection removes irrelevant attributes. feature extraction creates useful features. dimensionality reduction simplifies datasets. handling imbalanced data ensures balanced learning.

other important devices include modems, which convert digital signals to analog for transmission over telephone or cable lines and vice versa, enabling internet access; access points, which allow wireless devices to connect to a wired network via wi-fi; and firewalls, which monitor and control network traffic to block unauthorized access and protect against cyber threats. modern networks often use combinations of these devices to provide reliable, efficient, and secure connectivity. in short, network devices are essential for enabling communication, managing traffic, and maintaining the performance and security of networks.

memory protection in paged environment: memory protection in a paged environment is accomplished by protection bits that are associated with each frame. these bits are kept in the page table. one bit can define a page to be read-write or read-only. this protection bit can be checked to verify that no writes are being made to a read-only page. an attempt to write to a read- only page causes a hardware trap to the operating system (or memory-protection violation). page 60  one more bit is attached to each entry in the page table: a valid-invalid bit. when this bit is set to "valid," this value indicates that the associated page is in the process' logical- address space, and is a legal (or valid) page. if the bit is set to "invalid," this value indicates that the page is not in the process' logical-address space.  illegal addresses are trapped by using the valid-invalid bit. the operating system sets this bit for each page to allow or disallow accesses to that page.

in fcfs scheduling the process which arrives first in the ready queue is firstly assigned the cpu. in case of a tie, process with smaller process id is executed first. it is always non-preemptive in nature. jobs are executed on first come, first serve basis. it is a non-preemptive, pre-emptive scheduling algorithm. easy to understand and implement. its implementation is based on fifo queue. poor in performance as average wait time is high.

memory-efficient training techniques such as gradient checkpointing, mixed-precision computation, and activation recomputation enable training of extremely large models on limited hardware. these methods reduce memory footprint while preserving performance, making cutting-edge deep learning accessible to research labs and enterprises without massive gpu clusters.

feature engineering enhances machine learning performance. feature selection identifies important features. feature extraction transforms data. dimensionality reduction reduces complexity. handling imbalanced datasets improves predictions.

the name "telnet" refers to two things: a protocol specifying how two parties are to communicate and a software application that implements the protocol as a service. user data is interspersed in-band with telnet control information in an 8-bit byte oriented data connection over the transmission control protocol (tcp). telnet transmits all information including usernames and passwords in plaintext so it is not recommended for security-sensitive applications such as remote management of routers.telnet's use for this purpose has waned significantly in favor of ssh. some extensions to telnet which would provide encryption have been proposed.

the data link layer supports reliable communication between neighboring devices connected to the same network medium. framing encapsulates network-layer packets into frames before transmission across the physical channel. error detection and correction mechanisms detect damaged frames during transmission and help maintain data integrity. flow control coordinates communication speed between sending and receiving devices. mac addressing uniquely identifies each network interface card. switching concepts enable switches to analyze mac addresses and forward frames accurately within a network segment.

layering models in the early days of networking, before the rise of the ubiquitous internet, the international organization for standardization (iso) developed a layering model whose terminology persists today. the original 7-layer model has since been revised into the 5-layer tcp/ip reference model to better meet the current needs of protocol designers and the architecture of the modern internet. iso 7-layer reference model the following table describes the purpose of each layer within the original iso framework: layer 7application specifies how a particular application uses a network. layer 6presentation specifies how to represent data (encoding, encryption, etc.).layer 5sessionspecifies how to establish and manage communication with a remote system. layer 4transport specifies how to reliably handle data transfer (error recovery and flow control). layer 3network specifies addressing assignments and how packets are forwarded (routing). layer 2data link specifies the organization of data into frames and how to send them over a physical link. layer 1physical specifies the basic network hardware and the transmission of raw bits.

partially observable markov decision processes (pomdps) extend mdps to environments where the agent cannot fully observe the state. the agent maintains a belief state, representing a probability distribution over possible states. this increases complexity but reflects real-world scenarios more accurately. techniques such as recurrent neural networks are used to handle partial observability.

the osi model uses an approach called layering to illustrate and explain the message exchange process. this approach divides the various functions and services provided by a network into discrete groupings called layers. in the osi model, each layer provides services to the layer above, while hiding from that layer the processes used to implement the services. ideally, changes can be made to any layer without requiring changes to any of the other layers, as long as the inputs and outputs of the changed layer remain the same. for example, specifications for transmission over optical fiber cabling can be incorporated into layer 1 of an existing layer 2 network technology (e.g., ethernet) without modifying any of the existing layer 2 specifications. this makes it possible to take advantage of new technologies within a given layer without sacrificing compatibility with existing networks. the layers in the osi model are commonly described as being connected to each other in vertical form, also called a stack or protocol stack. the stack defines how network hardware and software interact at various levels to transfer messages between devices on a network and between networks on an internetwork. protocol stacks have the following characteristics: � � � � each layer provides a set of services. services of each layer and the stack are defined by protocols. lower layers provide services to upper layers. service access points (saps) are the connection points between layers. when a message needs to be transferred between two systems, a peer-to-peer relationship is established between the corresponding layers in the protocol stack of each system�a given layer at one end communicates with its counterpart at the other end over the network. the message, along with any control information, is passed down from the sending layer to the layer below. this process continues until the lowest layer in the stack is reached. the data is then transmitted from the lowest layer of the sending system to the lowest layer in the receiving system, where it is passed up through the layers until it reaches the counterpart, or peer, of the sending layers.

overfitting is a common issue where a model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen data. this occurs when the model learns noise instead of underlying patterns. on the other hand, underfitting happens when the model is too simple to capture the data structure. evaluation metrics and validation techniques are used to identify and address these problems, ensuring better generalization.

the network layer manages packet forwarding and routing between multiple interconnected networks. logical addressing is implemented using internet protocol version 4 and internet protocol version 6. ipv6 was introduced to overcome ipv4 address exhaustion and to support modern networking requirements. subnetting is a method used to divide networks into smaller segments by modifying the network and host portions of an ip address. this technique improves network performance and allows better utilization of address space. routing algorithms such as distance-vector and link-state determine optimal paths for packets. routers exchange routing updates using protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first, while inter-domain routing across the internet is handled by border gateway protocol.

the network layer manages packet delivery between networks through routing mechanisms and logical addressing. devices are assigned addresses using internet protocol version 4 or internet protocol version 6. ipv6 introduces a larger address space and more efficient routing features compared with ipv4. subnetting is used to divide large networks into smaller segments, improving traffic management and reducing congestion. routing algorithms determine optimal packet paths by analyzing link costs and network topology. routers exchange routing updates using protocols such as routing information protocol and open shortest path first. inter-domain routing across the internet is performed by border gateway protocol.

file a file is a named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage such as magnetic disks, magnetic tapes and optical disks. in general, a file is a sequence of bits, bytes, lines or records whose meaning is defined by the files creator and user. file structure a file structure should be according to a required format that the operating system can understand. a file has a certain defined structure according to its type. a text file is a sequence of characters organized into lines. a source file is a sequence of procedures and functions. an object file is a sequence of bytes organized into blocks that are understandable by the machine .when operating system defines different file structures, it also contains the code to support these file structure. unix, ms-dos support minimum number of file structure file type file type refers to the ability of the operating system to distinguish different types of file such as text files source files and binary files etc. many operating systems support many types of files.

the long-term scheduler, or admission scheduler, decides which jobs or processes are to be admitted to the ready queue (in main memory); that is, when an attempt is made to execute a program, its admission to the set of currently executing processes is either authorized or delayed by the long-term scheduler. thus, this scheduler dictates what processes are to run on a system, the degree of concurrency to be supported at any one time - whether many or few processes are to be executed concurrently, and how the split between i/o-intensive and cpu-intensive processes is to be handled. the long-term scheduler is responsible for controlling the degree of multiprogramming.

reliable data communication across a network link depends on the data link layer. framing converts raw bit streams into frames with defined boundaries. error detection and correction mechanisms verify frame integrity. flow control regulates the rate of communication between devices. mac addressing uniquely identifies network interfaces. switching concepts allow switches to forward frames efficiently.

this edition provides expanded explanations of cpu scheduling algorithms including preemptive and non-preemptive techniques. it explains process hierarchies, thread models, and context switching overhead. the book discusses synchronization problems and deadlock handling methods in detail. diagrams and solved examples help clarify process coordination mechanisms.

the application layer provides network services directly to end-user applications. one of the most widely used protocols at this layer is hypertext transfer protocol. http is responsible for transferring web pages and related resources between web browsers and web servers. it follows a request-response communication model where the client sends a request and the server responds with the requested content. http messages contain headers and a body that carries the data. because http is stateless, each request is processed independently without storing previous interactions. this simplicity allows http to scale efficiently across the global internet and support billions of web transactions every day.

internet protocol (ip) ip is the primary layer 3 protocol used to forward datagrams across the internet. routing: ip uses routing tables to determine the best path for data. these tables are prepared by routing protocols such as: ospf (open shortest path first) rip (routing information protocol) service type: ip provides a connectionless service. unlike connection-oriented circuits, it does not establish a dedicated path before sending data; instead, each packet is routed independently.

ensemble learning is a technique that combines predictions from multiple machine learning models to produce a more accurate and stable output. the underlying idea is that individual models may make errors, but combining them can cancel out these mistakes. bagging works by creating multiple datasets through bootstrap sampling and training independent models on each subset, reducing variance. boosting follows a sequential approach where each model corrects the errors of the previous one, reducing bias. gradient boosting enhances boosting by optimizing a loss function using gradient descent. xgboost is an advanced implementation that improves speed, handles missing data efficiently, and includes regularization to prevent overfitting.

this book explains how the linux kernel manages processes at a low level. it covers task structures, process descriptors, and scheduling classes. the completely fair scheduler (cfs) is discussed in detail, along with priority handling and load balancing. the book explains process states, context switching, and kernel preemption. it also describes synchronization tools such as spinlocks, semaphores, and mutexes. multiprocessing and interrupt handling are analyzed. this text provides practical knowledge of how linux internally manages multitasking systems and ensures efficient cpu utilization.
