the performance of machine learning algorithms depends heavily on the quality of input features. feature engineering focuses on preparing and transforming raw data into forms that algorithms can understand effectively. one key step is feature selection, which identifies the most relevant variables and removes redundant or irrelevant attributes. this helps reduce computational complexity and prevents models from learning noise. feature extraction is another technique where existing attributes are transformed into new features using mathematical or statistical methods. dimensionality reduction is often required when datasets contain hundreds or thousands of variables, making analysis difficult. by reducing feature space, algorithms can operate more efficiently and generalize better. additionally, real-world datasets often suffer from class imbalance, where one category significantly outweighs others. handling imbalanced data through resampling, weighting, or synthetic data generation helps create models that make balanced and accurate predictions.

stallings explains context switching within the framework of process state transitions and scheduling strategies. he describes how the operating system must save the current process context-including program counter, cpu registers, stack pointer, and status flags-into the process control block (pcb) before selecting another process from the ready queue. subtopics discussed include dispatcher functionality, interrupt handling, and scheduling overhead. the book highlights voluntary versus involuntary context switching and explains how time slicing in preemptive systems increases switching frequency. stallings also discusses multiprocessor scheduling, where switching decisions must consider processor affinity and cache performance, emphasizing that excessive context switching reduces overall throughput.

describes context switching within the broader discussion of concurrency and scheduling. when a process voluntarily yields the cpu or is preempted, the os kernel intervenes to preserve its computational context. the saved state ensures that execution can resume exactly where it left off. the book explains that context switching overhead increases in systems with complex memory hierarchies, as caches and tlb entries may need updating. proper synchronization mechanisms and balanced scheduling policies reduce unnecessary switching.

deadlock characterization deadlock can arise if four conditions hold simultaneously. 1. mutual exclusion: only one process at a time can use a resource. 2. hold and wait: a process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes. 3. no preemption: a resource can be released only voluntarily by the process holding it, after that process has completed its task. 4. circular wait: there exists a set {p0, p  p0} of waiting processes such that p0 is waiting for a resource that is held by p1, p1 is waiting for a resource that is held by p  pn-1 is waiting for a resource that is held by pn, and p0 is waiting for a resource that is held by p0.

on a modern computer, when a user clicks on a file or application icon, the application starts up. how does this happen and who is called? of course, we could implement everything that needs to happen in the kernel - draw the icon for every item in the file system, map mouse positions to the intended icon, catch the mouse click, and start the process. in early batch processing systems, the kernel was in control by necessity. users submitted jobs, and the operating system took it from there, instantiating the process when it was time to run the job. a different approach is to allow user programs to create and manage their own processes. this has fostered a blizzard of innovation. today, programs that create and manage processes include window managers, web servers, web browsers, shell command line interpreters, source code control systems, databases, compilers, and document preparation systems. we could go on, but you get the idea. if creating a process is something a process can do, then anyone can build a new version of any of these applications, without recompiling the kernel or forcing anyone else to use it. an early motivation for user-level process management was to allow developers to write their own shell command line interpreters. a shell is a job control system; both windows and unix have a shell. many tasks involve a sequence of steps to do something, each of which can be its own program. with a shell, you can write down the sequence of steps, as a sequence of programs to run to do each step. thus, you can view it as a very early version of a scripting system. process management is a core operating system function responsible for handling the execution of programs. a process is defined as a program in execution and includes the program counter, registers, stack, and data section. the operating system maintains this information using a process control block (pcb), which allows it to track the process state, schedule cpu time, and manage context switches. through scheduling and synchronization mechanisms, the os enables multiple processes to execute concurrently while ensuring system correctness and efficiency.

physical layer this layer is the lowest layer in the osi model. it helps in the transmission of data between two machines that are communicating through a physical medium, which can be optical fibers ,copper wire or wireless etc. the following are the main functions of the physical layer: hardware specification: the details of the physical cables, network interface cards, wireless radios, etc are a part of this layer. encoding and signaling: how are the bits encoded in the medium is also decided by this layer. for example, on the copper wire medium, we can use different voltage levels for a certain time interval to represent '0' and '1'. we may use +5mv for 1nsec to represent '1' and -5mv for 1nsec to represent '0'. all the issues of modulation is dealt with in this layer. eg, we may use binary phase shift keying for the representation of '1' and '0' rather than using different voltage levels if we have to transfer in rf waves. binary phase shift keying data transmission and reception: the transfer of each bit of data is the responsibility of this layer. this layer assures the transmission of each bit with a high probability. the transmission of the bits is not completely reliable as their is no error correction in this layer.

synchronization in operating systems ensures that multiple processes or threads do not corrupt shared kernel or user data while executing concurrently. in systems like minix, kernel-level synchronization is necessary because interrupts, system calls, and background processes may operate simultaneously. mutual exclusion mechanisms such as locks protect critical kernel data structures like process tables and memory maps. without synchronization, concurrent access could produce inconsistent system states. the text explains that atomic operations at the hardware level form the base for building higher-level primitives. efficient synchronization must minimize overhead while guaranteeing correctness, especially in multiprocessor systems.

memory-mapped files provide a mechanism for a process to access files by directly incorporating file data into the process address space. the use of mapped files can significantly reduce i/o data movement since the file data does not have to be copied into process data buffers, as is done by the read and write subroutines. when more than one process maps the same file, its contents are shared among them, providing a low-overhead mechanism by which processes can synchronize and communicate.

principle of complete mediation. every access to every resource should be checked for authority. it implies that we must have a way to determine the source of a request (the requester). principle of least authority. this principle, often known as pola, states that any (sub) system should have just enough authority (privilege) to perform its task and no more. thus, if attackers compromise such a system, they elevate their privilege by only the bare minimum.

a bridge is a data link layer device that interconnects two different lans using the same protocol. bridges are like switches as they can perform intelligent packet forwarding by comparing the source and destination mac address. however, unlike switch, the bridge has a single input and output port, making it less effective for network communication.

starvation a problem closely related to deadlock and livelock is starvation. in a dynamic system, requests for resources happen all the time. some policy is needed to make a decision about who gets which resource when. this policy, although seemingly reasonable, may lead to some processes never getting service even though they are not deadlocked.as an example, consider allocation of the printer. imagine that the system uses some algorithm to ensure that allocating the printer does not lead to deadlock. now suppose that several processes all want it at once. who should get it?one possible allocation algorithm is to give it to the process with the smallestfile to print (assuming this information is available). this approach maximizes thenumber of happy customers and seems fair. now consider what happens in a busy system when one process has a huge file to print. every time the printer is free, the system will look around and choose the process with the shortest file. if there is a constant stream of processes with short files, the process with the huge file will never be allocated the printer. it will simply starve to death (be postponed indefinitely, even though it is not blocked). starvation can be avoided by using a first-come, first-served resource allocation policy. with this approach, the process waiting the longest gets served next. in due course of time, any giv en process will eventually become the oldest and thus get the needed resource. it is worth mentioning that some people do not make a distinction between starvation and deadlock because in both cases there is no forward progress. others feel that they are fundamentally different because a process could easily be programmed to try to do something n times and, if all of them failed, try something else. a blocked process does not have that choice.

the transport layer enables end-to-end communication between application processes across a network. it ensures that data transmitted by the sender is correctly received by the destination application. tcp offers reliable data transfer using techniques such as sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmissions. udp provides a lightweight communication method without reliability mechanisms, making it suitable for applications requiring fast transmission. flow control manages the rate of data transmission between sender and receiver to avoid buffer overflow. congestion control is implemented mainly in tcp to maintain network stability during heavy traffic conditions. port numbers play a vital role in identifying services like web servers, email servers, and file transfer systems.

when two processes communicate using shared memory, synchronization is required to guarantee memory consistency. message passing subsumes communication, buffering, and synchronization. multiprocessor operating systems have experimented with a variety of communication abstractions, including ports, mailboxes, and links, which are kernel-handled message buffers that may be unidirectional or bidirectional.

within networking systems, the data link layer performs several key tasks to ensure reliable communication. framing divides transmitted bits into frames with headers and trailers. error detection and correction mechanisms detect corrupted frames and allow retransmission if necessary. flow control prevents data overflow at the receiver. mac addressing uniquely identifies network devices. switching concepts allow switches to forward frames based on mac address information.

milenkovic describes context switching as the cornerstone of time-sharing systems. it allows multiple processes to share a single cpu by preserving execution contexts. the os must carefully manage context switching to ensure fairness and efficiency. while context switching enables multitasking, excessive switching can reduce throughput. the book emphasizes optimizing scheduling to reduce unnecessary switches.

in this edition, nutt analyzes context switching in terms of system performance metrics such as throughput and turnaround time. the scheduler's policy directly influences how often switches occur. when a switch happens, kernel routines save processor registers and update accounting data. the author explains that context switching not only involves cpu state but may also require memory management unit (mmu) updates, especially when switching between processes with separate address spaces. the book stresses that modern systems optimize switching by reducing kernel entry costs and improving data structure efficiency.

the data link layer provides link-level reliability in computer networks. framing organizes bits into frames containing control information. error detection and correction techniques detect transmission errors. flow control prevents excessive data transmission. mac addressing uniquely identifies devices in a lan. switching concepts allow switches to route frames efficiently.

this book explores process management within macos (formerly mac os x). it explains how the xnu kernel manages tasks, threads, and scheduling policies. the author discusses mach-based process abstractions, interprocess communication via mach ports, and signal handling. it also covers priority-based scheduling and kernel-level synchronization. the book highlights hybrid kernel architecture and explains how different subsystems coordinate to manage processes efficiently. practical examples illustrate debugging and monitoring process behavior in macos systems.

the firewall acts as a packet filter. it inspects each and every incoming and outgoing packet. packets meeting some criterion described in rules formulated by the network administrator are forwarded normally. those that fail the test are unceremoniously dropped.

synchronization mechanisms regulate concurrent process execution to preserve system consistency.synchronization enforces mutual exclusion and event ordering, ensuring fairness among competing processes.synchronization coordinates shared resource usage, prevents race conditions, and ensures orderly concurrent execution in multitasking environments.

pipes are unidirectional; one end must be designated as the reading end and the other as the writing end. note that there is no restriction that different processes must read from and write to the pipe; rather, if one process writes to a pipe then immediately reads from it, the process will receive its own message. if two processes need to exchange messages back and forth, they should use two pipes.

unguided media unguided media, also known as wireless or unbounded transmission media , uses electromagnetic waves to transmit data without any physical medium. signals propagate through free space such as air or vacuum. the main types of unguided media are radio waves, microwaves, and infrared waves.
